title: Durable Counter
description: We build a counter that ticks once per second and survives restarts. By the end you have a running app whose state lives in a file ledger and outlives the process that created it.
run_intro: The app lives in <0>libs/examples/apps/durable-counter</0>. Start it from that directory:
run_output_intro: The count ticks up once per second:
run_loop: Now kill it. Stop the process with <0>Ctrl+C</0>, run <1>npm start</1> again, and watch the counter restore the last persisted value and keep counting from there:
run_note: There is no watch flag here. Plain <0>creact index.tsx</0> converges the tree once, and the interval inside the handler keeps the process alive until you stop it. The rest of this page walks through every line that makes the resume possible.
entry_intro: Every CReact app exports a default async function that the <0>creact</0> CLI calls. Ours wires a component tree to a persistence backend and a stack name:
entry_notice: Notice the three arguments to <0>render</0>: a function returning JSX, a <1>Memory</1> implementation, and the stack name <2>durable-counter</2>. The stack name is the ledger key — whatever state this run saves under it is what the next run restores.
file_memory_intro: <0>Memory</0> is the persistence contract — two methods, <1>getState</1> and <2>saveState</2>. This implementation keeps one JSON file per stack under the directory you pass it:
file_memory_notice: That is the entire durability layer. <0>getState</0> returns null when no ledger exists yet, and <1>saveState</1> writes the full deployment state after every change. Swap this class for DynamoDB or Postgres and nothing else in the app changes.
handler_intro: The handler is where durable state meets a live side effect. It restores the last count, then ticks it forward once per second:
handler_notice: <0>setOutputs</0> works like React's setState — the updater receives <1>prev</1>, the last persisted value, even across restarts. The first call re-publishes the saved count, or starts at zero on a fresh ledger.
handler_rerun_notice: Handlers re-run on every startup. The interval does not survive a restart, so the handler re-establishes it each time, and the function it returns is cleanup — the runtime calls it on dispose and before re-running the handler.
counter_intro: A component claims the handler with <0>useAsyncOutput</0> and reacts to its outputs:
counter_notice: <0>counter.count</0> is a reactive accessor, so the <1>createEffect</1> re-runs on every tick and prints the value. The component body itself runs once — reactivity comes from signals and effects, not re-rendering.
app_intro: The root component declares the resource tree:
app_notice: The <0>key</0> is required on every element whose component calls <1>useAsyncOutput</1>. Path plus key is the node's durable identity — rename the key and the runtime treats it as a brand-new resource with a fresh count.
recap_durable_outputs: Outputs published with <0>setOutputs</0> persist to Memory and hydrate on the next run.
recap_prev: <0>setOutputs(prev => …)</0> receives the last persisted value, even across restarts.
recap_rerun: Handlers re-run on every startup to re-establish side effects like intervals.
recap_cleanup: A function returned from the handler is cleanup, called on dispose and before a re-run.
try_it: Change the interval to 250 milliseconds and restart to watch the count accelerate from where it left off, then delete <0>.state</0> and watch it start over at zero.
